revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Murai Kodo]][[ja:村井古道]]
{{Infobox person|birth_date=1681|death_date={{death date|1749|11|23}}|birth_place={{ill|Higashijōdochō|qid=Q85881228}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|haiku poet|qid=Q11386386}}; {{ill|local historian|qid=Q1595570}}; {{ill|surgeon|qid=Q774306}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q96711987]]
Wikidata name: Murai Kodō
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Kodō Murai''' (村井 古道, [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year ([[:en:1681|1681]]) – [[:en:Kan'en|Kan'en]] 2nd year [[October 14 (Lunisolar)|October 14]] ([[:en:November 23|November 23]], [[:en:1749|1749]])) was a [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:local historian|local historian]] of [[Nara]], a [[:en:surgeon|surgeon]], and a [[haiku poet]].

His representative work is the ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}}''.

== Biography ==

=== Early Life ===
Kodō Murai was born in [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year (1681) in {{ill|Tōjōdo-chō|ja|東城戸町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōjōdo-chō|qq=|1=Higashijōdochō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85881228}}, Nara. His father was Michihiro Murai, the author of ''{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū|ja|南都名所集|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and also a haiku poet. Kodō later lived in {{ill|Nishijōdo-chō|ja|西城戸町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishijōdo-chō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for much of his life.{{sfn|{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja|『俳諧今宮草』の編者無名園古道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|p=401}} His real name was Katsukuro, and as a surgeon, he used the name Shōtetsu.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=401}} Kodō’s most renowned pen name was "Mumeien Kodō," but he also used many other pseudonyms, including ''Dōsei,'' ''Suiko,'' ''Itsukawa Masuhito,'' ''Seifūken Murai Dōsei,'' and ''Sasagusa Sanjin Gengentoku Kodō.''{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=399}}

=== Work as a Haiku Poet ===
Murai was a disciple of {{ill|Konishi Raizan|ja|小西来山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konishi Raizan|qq=|1=Konishi Raizan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463609}}, editing Raizan’s posthumous collection, ''{{ill|Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja|俳諧今宮草|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' and contributing to various haiku anthologies. However, despite his early exposure to haiku through his father, Murai's natural aptitude for the art form was limited, and his achievements as a haiku poet were overshadowed by his greater contributions as a local historian.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=381, 392}}

=== Interest in Local History ===
At around the age of 20, Murai became interested in local history, producing works such as his magnum opus, ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}},'' which represented a significant departure from the tradition of temple- and shrine-focused local histories.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=58}} His works incorporated a scientific approach, citing sources meticulously and critically evaluating oral traditions.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=414}}

Kodō's historical writings were considered revolutionary in Nara’s historiography. Previous works, such as ''{{ill|Nara-zoku|ja|奈良曝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara-zoku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Yamato Meisho-ki|ja|大和名所記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Meisho-ki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' often lacked rigor, relying heavily on oral accounts or focusing predominantly on religious sites.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|p=414}} Kodō, however, expanded his scope to include the origins of towns and other secular topics, making ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' the first comprehensive topographical history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=413, 414}}

== Legacy and Rediscovery ==
Kodō’s works were often attributed incorrectly to his contemporary, {{ill|Hiramatsu Baichi|ja|平松倍千|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Baichi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leading to centuries of confusion about his identity. This misattribution was only corrected in the mid-20th century when researchers such as Kanai Toranosuke identified Kodō as the true author of ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' and other important historical texts.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=389}}

== Works ==
=== Historical Writings ===
* ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' (奈良坊目拙解) – Murai's magnum opus and the first scientific local history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=458}}
* ''Nanto Meisho Junro Ryaku-shi'' (南都名所順路略志) – A concise guide to Nara's famous sites.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=394}}
* ''Nara Nenchū Gyōji'' (奈良年中行事) – A detailed calendar of annual events in Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=394, 395}}

=== Haiku Collections ===
* ''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa'' (俳諧今宮草) – A collection of posthumous works by Konishi Raizan, co-edited by Murai.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=381}}

Kodō Murai was born in [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year (1681) in {{ill|Tōjōdo-chō|ja|東城戸町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōjōdo-chō|qq=|1=Higashijōdochō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85881228}}, Nara. His father was Michihiro Murai, the author of ''{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū|ja|南都名所集|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and also a haiku poet. Kodō later lived in {{ill|Nishijōdo-chō|ja|西城戸町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishijōdo-chō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for much of his life.{{sfn|{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja|『俳諧今宮草』の編者無名園古道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|p=401}} His real name was Katsukuro, and as a surgeon, he used the name Shōtetsu.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=401}} Kodō’s most renowned pen name was "Mumeien Kodō," but he also used many other pseudonyms, including ''Dōsei,'' ''Suiko,'' ''Itsukawa Masuhito,'' ''Seifūken Murai Dōsei,'' and ''Sasagusa Sanjin Gengentoku Kodō.''{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=399}}

=== Work as a Haiku Poet ===
Murai was a disciple of {{ill|Konishi Raizan|ja|小西来山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konishi Raizan|qq=|1=Konishi Raizan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463609}}, editing Raizan’s posthumous collection, ''{{ill|Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja|俳諧今宮草|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' and contributing to various haiku anthologies. However, despite his early exposure to haiku through his father, Murai's natural aptitude for the art form was limited, and his achievements as a haiku poet were overshadowed by his greater contributions as a local historian.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=381, 392}}

=== Interest in Local History ===
At around the age of 20, Murai became interested in local history, producing works such as his magnum opus, ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}},'' which represented a significant departure from the tradition of temple- and shrine-focused local histories.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=58}} His works incorporated a scientific approach, citing sources meticulously and critically evaluating oral traditions.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=414}}

Kodō's historical writings were considered revolutionary in Nara’s historiography. Previous works, such as ''{{ill|Nara-zoku|ja|奈良曝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara-zoku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Yamato Meisho-ki|ja|大和名所記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Meisho-ki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' often lacked rigor, relying heavily on oral accounts or focusing predominantly on religious sites.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|p=414}} Kodō, however, expanded his scope to include the origins of towns and other secular topics, making ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' the first comprehensive topographical history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=413, 414}}

== Legacy and Rediscovery ==
Kodō’s works were often attributed incorrectly to his contemporary, {{ill|Hiramatsu Baichi|ja|平松倍千|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Baichi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leading to centuries of confusion about his identity. This misattribution was only corrected in the mid-20th century when researchers such as Kanai Toranosuke identified Kodō as the true author of ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' and other important historical texts.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=389}}

== Works ==
=== Historical Writings ===
* ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' (奈良坊目拙解) – Murai's magnum opus and the first scientific local history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=458}}
* ''Nanto Meisho Junro Ryaku-shi'' (南都名所順路略志) – A concise guide to Nara's famous sites.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=394}}
* ''Nara Nenchū Gyōji'' (奈良年中行事) – A detailed calendar of annual events in Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=394, 395}}

=== Haiku Collections ===
* ''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa'' (俳諧今宮草) – A collection of posthumous works by Konishi Raizan, co-edited by Murai.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=381}}

Kodō later lived in {{ill|Nishijōdo-chō|ja|西城戸町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishijōdo-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for much of his life.{{sfn|{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja|『俳諧今宮草』の編者無名園古道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|p=401}} His real name was Katsukuro, and as a surgeon, he used the name Shōtetsu.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=401}} Kodō’s most renowned pen name was "Mumeien Kodō," but he also used many other pseudonyms, including ''Dōsei,'' ''Suiko,'' ''Itsukawa Masuhito,'' ''Seifūken Murai Dōsei,'' and ''Sasagusa Sanjin Gengentoku Kodō.''{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=399}}

=== Work as a Haiku Poet ===
Murai was a disciple of {{ill|Konishi Raizan|ja|小西来山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konishi Raizan|1=Konishi Raizan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463609}}, editing Raizan’s posthumous collection, ''{{ill|Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja|俳諧今宮草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' and contributing to various haiku anthologies. However, despite his early exposure to haiku through his father, Murai's natural aptitude for the art form was limited, and his achievements as a haiku poet were overshadowed by his greater contributions as a local historian.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=381, 392}}

=== Interest in Local History ===
At around the age of 20, Murai became interested in local history, producing works such as his magnum opus, ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}},'' which represented a significant departure from the tradition of temple- and shrine-focused local histories.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=58}} His works incorporated a scientific approach, citing sources meticulously and critically evaluating oral traditions.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=414}}

Kodō's historical writings were considered revolutionary in Nara’s historiography. Previous works, such as ''{{ill|Nara-zoku|ja|奈良曝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara-zoku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Yamato Meisho-ki|ja|大和名所記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Meisho-ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' often lacked rigor, relying heavily on oral accounts or focusing predominantly on religious sites.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|p=414}} Kodō, however, expanded his scope to include the origins of towns and other secular topics, making ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' the first comprehensive topographical history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=413, 414}}

== Legacy and Rediscovery ==
Kodō’s works were often attributed incorrectly to his contemporary, {{ill|Hiramatsu Baichi|ja|平松倍千|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Baichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leading to centuries of confusion about his identity. This misattribution was only corrected in the mid-20th century when researchers such as Kanai Toranosuke identified Kodō as the true author of ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' and other important historical texts.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=389}}

== Works ==
=== Historical Writings ===
* ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' (奈良坊目拙解) – Murai's magnum opus and the first scientific local history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=458}}
* ''Nanto Meisho Junro Ryaku-shi'' (南都名所順路略志) – A concise guide to Nara's famous sites.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=394}}
* ''Nara Nenchū Gyōji'' (奈良年中行事) – A detailed calendar of annual events in Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=394, 395}}

=== Haiku Collections ===
* ''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa'' (俳諧今宮草) – A collection of posthumous works by Konishi Raizan, co-edited by Murai.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=381}}

'''Kodō Murai''' (村井 古道, [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year ([[:en:1681|1681]]) – [[:en:Kan'en|Kan'en]] 2nd year [[October 14 (Lunisolar)|October 14]] ([[:en:November 23|November 23]], [[:en:1749|1749]])) was a [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:local historian|local historian]] of [[Nara]], a [[:en:surgeon|surgeon]], and a [[haiku poet]].

His representative work is the ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}}''.

== Biography ==

=== Early Life ===
Kodō Murai was born in [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year (1681) in {{ill|Tōjōdo-chō|ja|東城戸町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōjōdo-chō|1=Higashijōdochō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85881228}}, Nara. His father was Michihiro Murai, the author of ''{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū|ja|南都名所集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and also a haiku poet. Kodō later lived in {{ill|Nishijōdo-chō|ja|西城戸町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishijōdo-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for much of his life.{{sfn|{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja|『俳諧今宮草』の編者無名園古道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|p=401}} His real name was Katsukuro, and as a surgeon, he used the name Shōtetsu.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=401}} Kodō’s most renowned pen name was "Mumeien Kodō," but he also used many other pseudonyms, including ''Dōsei,'' ''Suiko,'' ''Itsukawa Masuhito,'' ''Seifūken Murai Dōsei,'' and ''Sasagusa Sanjin Gengentoku Kodō.''{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=399}}

Murai was a disciple of {{ill|Konishi Raizan|ja|小西来山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konishi Raizan|1=Konishi Raizan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463609}}, editing Raizan’s posthumous collection, ''{{ill|Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja|俳諧今宮草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' and contributing to various haiku anthologies. However, despite his early exposure to haiku through his father, Murai's natural aptitude for the art form was limited, and his achievements as a haiku poet were overshadowed by his greater contributions as a local historian.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=381, 392}}

=== Interest in Local History ===
At around the age of 20, Murai became interested in local history, producing works such as his magnum opus, ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}},'' which represented a significant departure from the tradition of temple- and shrine-focused local histories.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=58}} His works incorporated a scientific approach, citing sources meticulously and critically evaluating oral traditions.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=414}}

Kodō's historical writings were considered revolutionary in Nara’s historiography. Previous works, such as ''{{ill|Nara-zoku|ja|奈良曝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara-zoku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Yamato Meisho-ki|ja|大和名所記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Meisho-ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' often lacked rigor, relying heavily on oral accounts or focusing predominantly on religious sites.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|p=414}} Kodō, however, expanded his scope to include the origins of towns and other secular topics, making ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' the first comprehensive topographical history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=413, 414}}

== Legacy and Rediscovery ==
Kodō’s works were often attributed incorrectly to his contemporary, {{ill|Hiramatsu Baichi|ja|平松倍千|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Baichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leading to centuries of confusion about his identity. This misattribution was only corrected in the mid-20th century when researchers such as Kanai Toranosuke identified Kodō as the true author of ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' and other important historical texts.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=389}}

However, despite his early exposure to haiku through his father, Murai's natural aptitude for the art form was limited, and his achievements as a haiku poet were overshadowed by his greater contributions as a local historian.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=381, 392}}

=== Interest in Local History ===
At around the age of 20, Murai became interested in local history, producing works such as his magnum opus, ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}},'' which represented a significant departure from the tradition of temple- and shrine-focused local histories.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=58}} His works incorporated a scientific approach, citing sources meticulously and critically evaluating oral traditions.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=414}}

Kodō's historical writings were considered revolutionary in Nara’s historiography. Previous works, such as ''{{ill|Nara-zoku|ja|奈良曝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara-zoku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Yamato Meisho-ki|ja|大和名所記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Meisho-ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' often lacked rigor, relying heavily on oral accounts or focusing predominantly on religious sites.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|p=414}} Kodō, however, expanded his scope to include the origins of towns and other secular topics, making ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' the first comprehensive topographical history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=413, 414}}

== Legacy and Rediscovery ==
Kodō’s works were often attributed incorrectly to his contemporary, {{ill|Hiramatsu Baichi|ja|平松倍千|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Baichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leading to centuries of confusion about his identity. This misattribution was only corrected in the mid-20th century when researchers such as Kanai Toranosuke identified Kodō as the true author of ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' and other important historical texts.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=389}}

== Works ==
=== Historical Writings ===
* ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' (奈良坊目拙解) – Murai's magnum opus and the first scientific local history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=458}}
* ''Nanto Meisho Junro Ryaku-shi'' (南都名所順路略志) – A concise guide to Nara's famous sites.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=394}}
* ''Nara Nenchū Gyōji'' (奈良年中行事) – A detailed calendar of annual events in Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=394, 395}}

=== Haiku Collections ===
* ''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa'' (俳諧今宮草) – A collection of posthumous works by Konishi Raizan, co-edited by Murai.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=381}}

{{Infobox person|birth_date=1681|death_date={{death date|1749|11|23}}|birth_place={{ill|Higashijōdochō|WD=Q85881228}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|haiku poet|WD=Q11386386}}; {{ill|local historian|WD=Q1595570}}; {{ill|surgeon|WD=Q774306}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q96711987]]

'''Kodō Murai''' (村井 古道, [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year ([[:en:1681|1681]]) – [[:en:Kan'en|Kan'en]] 2nd year [[October 14 (Lunisolar)|October 14]] ([[:en:November 23|November 23]], [[:en:1749|1749]])) was a [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:local historian|local historian]] of [[Nara]], a [[:en:surgeon|surgeon]], and a [[haiku poet]].

His representative work is the ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}}''.

== Biography ==

=== Early Life ===
Kodō Murai was born in [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year (1681) in {{ill|Tōjōdo-chō|ja|東城戸町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōjōdo-chō|1=Higashijōdochō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85881228}}, Nara. His father was Michihiro Murai, the author of ''{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū|ja|南都名所集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and also a haiku poet. Kodō later lived in {{ill|Nishijōdo-chō|ja|西城戸町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishijōdo-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for much of his life.{{sfn|{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja|『俳諧今宮草』の編者無名園古道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|p=401}} His real name was Katsukuro, and as a surgeon, he used the name Shōtetsu.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=401}} Kodō’s most renowned pen name was "Mumeien Kodō," but he also used many other pseudonyms, including ''Dōsei,'' ''Suiko,'' ''Itsukawa Masuhito,'' ''Seifūken Murai Dōsei,'' and ''Sasagusa Sanjin Gengentoku Kodō.''{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=399}}

=== Work as a Haiku Poet ===
Murai was a disciple of {{ill|Konishi Raizan|ja|小西来山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konishi Raizan|1=Konishi Raizan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463609}}, editing Raizan’s posthumous collection, ''{{ill|Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja|俳諧今宮草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' and contributing to various haiku anthologies. However, despite his early exposure to haiku through his father, Murai's natural aptitude for the art form was limited, and his achievements as a haiku poet were overshadowed by his greater contributions as a local historian.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=381, 392}}

=== Interest in Local History ===
At around the age of 20, Murai became interested in local history, producing works such as his magnum opus, ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}},'' which represented a significant departure from the tradition of temple- and shrine-focused local histories.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=58}} His works incorporated a scientific approach, citing sources meticulously and critically evaluating oral traditions.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=414}}

Kodō's historical writings were considered revolutionary in Nara’s historiography. Previous works, such as ''{{ill|Nara-zoku|ja|奈良曝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara-zoku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Yamato Meisho-ki|ja|大和名所記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Meisho-ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' often lacked rigor, relying heavily on oral accounts or focusing predominantly on religious sites.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|p=414}} Kodō, however, expanded his scope to include the origins of towns and other secular topics, making ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' the first comprehensive topographical history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=413, 414}}

== Legacy and Rediscovery ==
Kodō’s works were often attributed incorrectly to his contemporary, {{ill|Hiramatsu Baichi|ja|平松倍千|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Baichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leading to centuries of confusion about his identity. This misattribution was only corrected in the mid-20th century when researchers such as Kanai Toranosuke identified Kodō as the true author of ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' and other important historical texts.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=389}}

* ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' (奈良坊目拙解) – Murai's magnum opus and the first scientific local history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=458}}
* ''Nanto Meisho Junro Ryaku-shi'' (南都名所順路略志) – A concise guide to Nara's famous sites.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=394}}
* ''Nara Nenchū Gyōji'' (奈良年中行事) – A detailed calendar of annual events in Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=394, 395}}

=== Haiku Collections ===

At around the age of 20, Murai became interested in local history, producing works such as his magnum opus, ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}},'' which represented a significant departure from the tradition of temple- and shrine-focused local histories.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=58}} His works incorporated a scientific approach, citing sources meticulously and critically evaluating oral traditions.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=414}}

Previous works, such as ''{{ill|Nara-zoku|ja|奈良曝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara-zoku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Yamato Meisho-ki|ja|大和名所記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Meisho-ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' often lacked rigor, relying heavily on oral accounts or focusing predominantly on religious sites.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|p=414}} Kodō, however, expanded his scope to include the origins of towns and other secular topics, making ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' the first comprehensive topographical history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=413, 414}}

== Legacy and Rediscovery ==
Kodō’s works were often attributed incorrectly to his contemporary, {{ill|Hiramatsu Baichi|ja|平松倍千|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Baichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leading to centuries of confusion about his identity. This misattribution was only corrected in the mid-20th century when researchers such as Kanai Toranosuke identified Kodō as the true author of ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' and other important historical texts.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=389}}

== Works ==
=== Historical Writings ===
* ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' (奈良坊目拙解) – Murai's magnum opus and the first scientific local history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=458}}
* ''Nanto Meisho Junro Ryaku-shi'' (南都名所順路略志) – A concise guide to Nara's famous sites.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=394}}
* ''Nara Nenchū Gyōji'' (奈良年中行事) – A detailed calendar of annual events in Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=394, 395}}

=== Haiku Collections ===
* ''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa'' (俳諧今宮草) – A collection of posthumous works by Konishi Raizan, co-edited by Murai.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=381}}

{{Infobox person|birth_date=1681|death_date={{death date|1749|11|23}}|birth_place={{ill|Higashijōdochō|WD=Q85881228}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|haiku poet|WD=Q11386386}}; {{ill|local historian|WD=Q1595570}}; {{ill|surgeon|WD=Q774306}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q96711987]]

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Kodō Murai''' (村井 古道, [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year ([[:en:1681|1681]]) – [[:en:Kan'en|Kan'en]] 2nd year [[October 14 (Lunisolar)|October 14]] ([[:en:November 23|November 23]], [[:en:1749|1749]])) was a [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:local historian|local historian]] of [[Nara]], a [[:en:surgeon|surgeon]], and a [[haiku poet]].

His representative work is the ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}}''.

== Biography ==

=== Early Life ===
Kodō Murai was born in [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year (1681) in {{ill|Tōjōdo-chō|ja|東城戸町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōjōdo-chō|1=Higashijōdochō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85881228}}, Nara. His father was Michihiro Murai, the author of ''{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū|ja|南都名所集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and also a haiku poet. Kodō later lived in {{ill|Nishijōdo-chō|ja|西城戸町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishijōdo-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for much of his life.{{sfn|{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja|『俳諧今宮草』の編者無名園古道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|p=401}} His real name was Katsukuro, and as a surgeon, he used the name Shōtetsu.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=401}} Kodō’s most renowned pen name was "Mumeien Kodō," but he also used many other pseudonyms, including ''Dōsei,'' ''Suiko,'' ''Itsukawa Masuhito,'' ''Seifūken Murai Dōsei,'' and ''Sasagusa Sanjin Gengentoku Kodō.''{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=399}}

=== Work as a Haiku Poet ===
Murai was a disciple of {{ill|Konishi Raizan|ja|小西来山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konishi Raizan|1=Konishi Raizan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463609}}, editing Raizan’s posthumous collection, ''{{ill|Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja|俳諧今宮草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' and contributing to various haiku anthologies. However, despite his early exposure to haiku through his father, Murai's natural aptitude for the art form was limited, and his achievements as a haiku poet were overshadowed by his greater contributions as a local historian.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=381, 392}}

=== Interest in Local History ===
At around the age of 20, Murai became interested in local history, producing works such as his magnum opus, ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}},'' which represented a significant departure from the tradition of temple- and shrine-focused local histories.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=58}} His works incorporated a scientific approach, citing sources meticulously and critically evaluating oral traditions.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=414}}

Kodō's historical writings were considered revolutionary in Nara’s historiography. Previous works, such as ''{{ill|Nara-zoku|ja|奈良曝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara-zoku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Yamato Meisho-ki|ja|大和名所記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Meisho-ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' often lacked rigor, relying heavily on oral accounts or focusing predominantly on religious sites.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|p=414}} Kodō, however, expanded his scope to include the origins of towns and other secular topics, making ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' the first comprehensive topographical history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=413, 414}}

== Legacy and Rediscovery ==
Kodō’s works were often attributed incorrectly to his contemporary, {{ill|Hiramatsu Baichi|ja|平松倍千|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Baichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leading to centuries of confusion about his identity. This misattribution was only corrected in the mid-20th century when researchers such as Kanai Toranosuke identified Kodō as the true author of ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' and other important historical texts.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=389}}

=== Historical Writings ===
* ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' (奈良坊目拙解) – Murai's magnum opus and the first scientific local history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=458}}
* ''Nanto Meisho Junro Ryaku-shi'' (南都名所順路略志) – A concise guide to Nara's famous sites.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=394}}
* ''Nara Nenchū Gyōji'' (奈良年中行事) – A detailed calendar of annual events in Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=394, 395}}

=== Haiku Collections ===
* ''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa'' (俳諧今宮草) – A collection of posthumous works by Konishi Raizan, co-edited by Murai.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=381}}

Kodō’s works were often attributed incorrectly to his contemporary, {{ill|Hiramatsu Baichi|ja|平松倍千|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Baichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leading to centuries of confusion about his identity. This misattribution was only corrected in the mid-20th century when researchers such as Kanai Toranosuke identified Kodō as the true author of ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' and other important historical texts.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=389}}

== Works ==
=== Historical Writings ===
* ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' (奈良坊目拙解) – Murai's magnum opus and the first scientific local history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=458}}
* ''Nanto Meisho Junro Ryaku-shi'' (南都名所順路略志) – A concise guide to Nara's famous sites.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=394}}
* ''Nara Nenchū Gyōji'' (奈良年中行事) – A detailed calendar of annual events in Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=394, 395}}

=== Haiku Collections ===
* ''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa'' (俳諧今宮草) – A collection of posthumous works by Konishi Raizan, co-edited by Murai.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=381}}

{{Infobox person|birth_date=1681|death_date={{death date|1749|11|23}}|birth_place={{ill|Higashijōdochō|WD=Q85881228}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|haiku poet|WD=Q11386386}}; {{ill|local historian|WD=Q1595570}}; {{ill|surgeon|WD=Q774306}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q96711987]]

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Kodō Murai''' (村井 古道, [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year ([[:en:1681|1681]]) – [[:en:Kan'en|Kan'en]] 2nd year [[October 14 (Lunisolar)|October 14]] ([[:en:November 23|November 23]], [[:en:1749|1749]])) was a [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:local historian|local historian]] of [[Nara]], a [[:en:surgeon|surgeon]], and a [[haiku poet]].

His representative work is the ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}}''.

== Biography ==

=== Early Life ===
Kodō Murai was born in [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year (1681) in {{ill|Tōjōdo-chō|ja|東城戸町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōjōdo-chō|1=Higashijōdochō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85881228}}, Nara. His father was Michihiro Murai, the author of ''{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū|ja|南都名所集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and also a haiku poet. Kodō later lived in {{ill|Nishijōdo-chō|ja|西城戸町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishijōdo-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for much of his life.{{sfn|{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja|『俳諧今宮草』の編者無名園古道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|p=401}} His real name was Katsukuro, and as a surgeon, he used the name Shōtetsu.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=401}} Kodō’s most renowned pen name was "Mumeien Kodō," but he also used many other pseudonyms, including ''Dōsei,'' ''Suiko,'' ''Itsukawa Masuhito,'' ''Seifūken Murai Dōsei,'' and ''Sasagusa Sanjin Gengentoku Kodō.''{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=399}}

=== Work as a Haiku Poet ===
Murai was a disciple of {{ill|Konishi Raizan|ja|小西来山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konishi Raizan|1=Konishi Raizan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463609}}, editing Raizan’s posthumous collection, ''{{ill|Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja|俳諧今宮草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' and contributing to various haiku anthologies. However, despite his early exposure to haiku through his father, Murai's natural aptitude for the art form was limited, and his achievements as a haiku poet were overshadowed by his greater contributions as a local historian.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=381, 392}}

=== Interest in Local History ===
At around the age of 20, Murai became interested in local history, producing works such as his magnum opus, ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}},'' which represented a significant departure from the tradition of temple- and shrine-focused local histories.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=58}} His works incorporated a scientific approach, citing sources meticulously and critically evaluating oral traditions.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=414}}

Kodō's historical writings were considered revolutionary in Nara’s historiography. Previous works, such as ''{{ill|Nara-zoku|ja|奈良曝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara-zoku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Yamato Meisho-ki|ja|大和名所記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Meisho-ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' often lacked rigor, relying heavily on oral accounts or focusing predominantly on religious sites.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|p=414}} Kodō, however, expanded his scope to include the origins of towns and other secular topics, making ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' the first comprehensive topographical history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=413, 414}}

== Legacy and Rediscovery ==
Kodō’s works were often attributed incorrectly to his contemporary, {{ill|Hiramatsu Baichi|ja|平松倍千|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Baichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leading to centuries of confusion about his identity. This misattribution was only corrected in the mid-20th century when researchers such as Kanai Toranosuke identified Kodō as the true author of ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' and other important historical texts.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=389}}

== Works ==
=== Historical Writings ===
* ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' (奈良坊目拙解) – Murai's magnum opus and the first scientific local history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=458}}
* ''Nanto Meisho Junro Ryaku-shi'' (南都名所順路略志) – A concise guide to Nara's famous sites.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=394}}
* ''Nara Nenchū Gyōji'' (奈良年中行事) – A detailed calendar of annual events in Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=394, 395}}

=== Haiku Collections ===
* ''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa'' (俳諧今宮草) – A collection of posthumous works by Konishi Raizan, co-edited by Murai.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=381}}

* ''Nanto Meisho Junro Ryaku-shi'' (南都名所順路略志) – A concise guide to Nara's famous sites.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=394}}
* ''Nara Nenchū Gyōji'' (奈良年中行事) – A detailed calendar of annual events in Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=394, 395}}

=== Haiku Collections ===
* ''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa'' (俳諧今宮草) – A collection of posthumous works by Konishi Raizan, co-edited by Murai.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=381}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Murai Kodo]][[ja:村井古道]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Murai Kodo]][[ja:村井古道]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Murai Kodo]][[ja:村井古道]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1681|death_date={{death date|1749|11|23}}|birth_place={{ill|Higashijōdochō|WD=Q85881228}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|haiku poet|WD=Q11386386}}; {{ill|local historian|WD=Q1595570}}; {{ill|surgeon|WD=Q774306}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q96711987]] Wikidata name: Murai Kodō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kodō Murai''' (村井 古道, [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year ([[:en:1681|1681]]) – [[:en:Kan'en|Kan'en]] 2nd year [[October 14 (Lunisolar)|October 14]] ([[:en:November 23|November 23]], [[:en:1749|1749]])) was a [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[:en:local historian|local historian]] of [[Nara]], a [[:en:surgeon|surgeon]], and a [[haiku poet]].
His representative work is the ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}}''.
== Biography ==
=== Early Life === Kodō Murai was born in [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year (1681) in {{ill|Tōjōdo-chō|ja|東城戸町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōjōdo-chō|1=Higashijōdochō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85881228}}, Nara. His father was Michihiro Murai, the author of ''{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū|ja|南都名所集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and also a haiku poet. Kodō later lived in {{ill|Nishijōdo-chō|ja|西城戸町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishijōdo-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for much of his life.{{sfn|{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja|『俳諧今宮草』の編者無名園古道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|p=401}} His real name was Katsukuro, and as a surgeon, he used the name Shōtetsu.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=401}} Kodō’s most renowned pen name was "Mumeien Kodō," but he also used many other pseudonyms, including ''Dōsei,'' ''Suiko,'' ''Itsukawa Masuhito,'' ''Seifūken Murai Dōsei,'' and ''Sasagusa Sanjin Gengentoku Kodō.''{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=399}}
=== Work as a Haiku Poet === Murai was a disciple of {{ill|Konishi Raizan|ja|小西来山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konishi Raizan|1=Konishi Raizan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463609}}, editing Raizan’s posthumous collection, ''{{ill|Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja|俳諧今宮草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' and contributing to various haiku anthologies. However, despite his early exposure to haiku through his father, Murai's natural aptitude for the art form was limited, and his achievements as a haiku poet were overshadowed by his greater contributions as a local historian.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=381, 392}}
=== Interest in Local History === At around the age of 20, Murai became interested in local history, producing works such as his magnum opus, ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}},'' which represented a significant departure from the tradition of temple- and shrine-focused local histories.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=58}} His works incorporated a scientific approach, citing sources meticulously and critically evaluating oral traditions.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=414}}
Kodō's historical writings were considered revolutionary in Nara’s historiography. Previous works, such as ''{{ill|Nara-zoku|ja|奈良曝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara-zoku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Yamato Meisho-ki|ja|大和名所記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Meisho-ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' often lacked rigor, relying heavily on oral accounts or focusing predominantly on religious sites.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|p=414}} Kodō, however, expanded his scope to include the origins of towns and other secular topics, making ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' the first comprehensive topographical history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=413, 414}}
== Legacy and Rediscovery == Kodō’s works were often attributed incorrectly to his contemporary, {{ill|Hiramatsu Baichi|ja|平松倍千|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Baichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leading to centuries of confusion about his identity. This misattribution was only corrected in the mid-20th century when researchers such as Kanai Toranosuke identified Kodō as the true author of ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' and other important historical texts.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=389}}
== Works == === Historical Writings === * ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' (奈良坊目拙解) – Murai's magnum opus and the first scientific local history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=458}} * ''Nanto Meisho Junro Ryaku-shi'' (南都名所順路略志) – A concise guide to Nara's famous sites.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=394}} * ''Nara Nenchū Gyōji'' (奈良年中行事) – A detailed calendar of annual events in Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=394, 395}}
=== Haiku Collections === * ''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa'' (俳諧今宮草) – A collection of posthumous works by Konishi Raizan, co-edited by Murai.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=381}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Murai Kodo]][[ja:村井古道]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Murai Kodo]][[ja:村井古道]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Murai Kodo]][[ja:村井古道]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1681|death_date={{death date|1749|11|23}}|birth_place={{ill|Higashijōdochō|WD=Q85881228}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|haiku poet|WD=Q11386386}}; {{ill|local historian|WD=Q1595570}}; {{ill|surgeon|WD=Q774306}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q96711987]] Wikidata name: Murai Kodō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kodō Murai''' (村井 古道, [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year ({{ill|1681|en|1681|WD=Q7645|lt=1681}}) – {{ill|Kan'en|en|Kan'en|WD=Q1145723|lt=Kan'en}} 2nd year [[October 14 (Lunisolar)|October 14]] ({{ill|November 23|en|November 23|WD=Q3021|lt=November 23}}, {{ill|1749|en|1749|WD=Q7607|lt=1749}})) was a {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} [[:en:local historian|local historian]] of [[Nara]], a {{ill|surgeon|en|surgeon|WD=Q774306|lt=surgeon}}, and a [[haiku poet]].
His representative work is the ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}}''.
== Biography ==
=== Early Life === Kodō Murai was born in [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year (1681) in {{ill|Tōjōdo-chō|ja|東城戸町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōjōdo-chō|1=Higashijōdochō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85881228}}, Nara. His father was Michihiro Murai, the author of ''{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū|ja|南都名所集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and also a haiku poet. Kodō later lived in {{ill|Nishijōdo-chō|ja|西城戸町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishijōdo-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for much of his life.{{sfn|{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja|『俳諧今宮草』の編者無名園古道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|p=401}} His real name was Katsukuro, and as a surgeon, he used the name Shōtetsu.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=401}} Kodō’s most renowned pen name was "Mumeien Kodō," but he also used many other pseudonyms, including ''Dōsei,'' ''Suiko,'' ''Itsukawa Masuhito,'' ''Seifūken Murai Dōsei,'' and ''Sasagusa Sanjin Gengentoku Kodō.''{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=399}}
=== Work as a Haiku Poet === Murai was a disciple of {{ill|Konishi Raizan|ja|小西来山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konishi Raizan|1=Konishi Raizan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463609}}, editing Raizan’s posthumous collection, ''{{ill|Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja|俳諧今宮草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' and contributing to various haiku anthologies. However, despite his early exposure to haiku through his father, Murai's natural aptitude for the art form was limited, and his achievements as a haiku poet were overshadowed by his greater contributions as a local historian.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=381, 392}}
=== Interest in Local History === At around the age of 20, Murai became interested in local history, producing works such as his magnum opus, ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}},'' which represented a significant departure from the tradition of temple- and shrine-focused local histories.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=58}} His works incorporated a scientific approach, citing sources meticulously and critically evaluating oral traditions.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=414}}
Kodō's historical writings were considered revolutionary in Nara’s historiography. Previous works, such as ''{{ill|Nara-zoku|ja|奈良曝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara-zoku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Yamato Meisho-ki|ja|大和名所記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Meisho-ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' often lacked rigor, relying heavily on oral accounts or focusing predominantly on religious sites.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|p=414}} Kodō, however, expanded his scope to include the origins of towns and other secular topics, making ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' the first comprehensive topographical history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=413, 414}}
== Legacy and Rediscovery == Kodō’s works were often attributed incorrectly to his contemporary, {{ill|Hiramatsu Baichi|ja|平松倍千|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Baichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leading to centuries of confusion about his identity. This misattribution was only corrected in the mid-20th century when researchers such as Kanai Toranosuke identified Kodō as the true author of ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' and other important historical texts.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=389}}
== Works == === Historical Writings === * ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' (奈良坊目拙解) – Murai's magnum opus and the first scientific local history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=458}} * ''Nanto Meisho Junro Ryaku-shi'' (南都名所順路略志) – A concise guide to Nara's famous sites.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=394}} * ''Nara Nenchū Gyōji'' (奈良年中行事) – A detailed calendar of annual events in Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=394, 395}}
=== Haiku Collections === * ''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa'' (俳諧今宮草) – A collection of posthumous works by Konishi Raizan, co-edited by Murai.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=381}}
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Nara-zoku|ja|奈良曝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara-zoku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Konishi Raizan|ja|小西来山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konishi Raizan|1=Konishi Raizan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463609}}

'''Kodō Murai''' (村井 古道, [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year ({{ill|1681|en|1681|WD=Q7645|lt=1681}}) – {{ill|Kan'en|en|Kan'en|WD=Q1145723|lt=Kan'en}} 2nd year [[October 14 (Lunisolar)|October 14]] ({{ill|November 23|en|November 23|WD=Q3021|lt=November 23}}, {{ill|1749|en|1749|WD=Q7607|lt=1749}})) was a {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} [[:en:local historian|local historian]] of [[Nara]], a {{ill|surgeon|en|surgeon|WD=Q774306|lt=surgeon}}, and a [[haiku poet]].

His representative work is the ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}}''.

== Biography ==

=== Early Life ===
Kodō Murai was born in [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year (1681) in {{ill|Tōjōdo-chō|ja|東城戸町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōjōdo-chō|1=Higashijōdochō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85881228}}, Nara. His father was Michihiro Murai, the author of ''{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū|ja|南都名所集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and also a haiku poet. Kodō later lived in {{ill|Nishijōdo-chō|ja|西城戸町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishijōdo-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for much of his life.{{sfn|{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja|『俳諧今宮草』の編者無名園古道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|p=401}} His real name was Katsukuro, and as a surgeon, he used the name Shōtetsu.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=401}} Kodō’s most renowned pen name was "Mumeien Kodō," but he also used many other pseudonyms, including ''Dōsei,'' ''Suiko,'' ''Itsukawa Masuhito,'' ''Seifūken Murai Dōsei,'' and ''Sasagusa Sanjin Gengentoku Kodō.''{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=399}}

=== Work as a Haiku Poet ===
Murai was a disciple of {{ill|Konishi Raizan|ja|小西来山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konishi Raizan|1=Konishi Raizan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463609}}, editing Raizan’s posthumous collection, ''{{ill|Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja|俳諧今宮草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' and contributing to various haiku anthologies. However, despite his early exposure to haiku through his father, Murai's natural aptitude for the art form was limited, and his achievements as a haiku poet were overshadowed by his greater contributions as a local historian.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=381, 392}}

=== Interest in Local History ===
At around the age of 20, Murai became interested in local history, producing works such as his magnum opus, ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}},'' which represented a significant departure from the tradition of temple- and shrine-focused local histories.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=58}} His works incorporated a scientific approach, citing sources meticulously and critically evaluating oral traditions.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=414}}

Kodō's historical writings were considered revolutionary in Nara’s historiography. Previous works, such as ''{{ill|Nara-zoku|ja|奈良曝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara-zoku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Yamato Meisho-ki|ja|大和名所記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Meisho-ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' often lacked rigor, relying heavily on oral accounts or focusing predominantly on religious sites.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|p=414}} Kodō, however, expanded his scope to include the origins of towns and other secular topics, making ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' the first comprehensive topographical history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=413, 414}}

== Legacy and Rediscovery ==
Kodō’s works were often attributed incorrectly to his contemporary, {{ill|Hiramatsu Baichi|ja|平松倍千|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Baichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leading to centuries of confusion about his identity. This misattribution was only corrected in the mid-20th century when researchers such as Kanai Toranosuke identified Kodō as the true author of ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' and other important historical texts.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=389}}

== Works ==
=== Historical Writings ===
* ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' (奈良坊目拙解) – Murai's magnum opus and the first scientific local history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=458}}
* ''Nanto Meisho Junro Ryaku-shi'' (南都名所順路略志) – A concise guide to Nara's famous sites.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=394}}
* ''Nara Nenchū Gyōji'' (奈良年中行事) – A detailed calendar of annual events in Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=394, 395}}

=== Haiku Collections ===
* ''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa'' (俳諧今宮草) – A collection of posthumous works by Konishi Raizan, co-edited by Murai.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=381}}

{{Infobox person|birth_date=1681|death_date={{death date|1749|11|23}}|birth_place={{ill|Higashijōdochō|WD=Q85881228}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|haiku poet|WD=Q11386386}}; {{ill|local historian|WD=Q1595570}}; {{ill|surgeon|WD=Q774306}}}}
'''Kodō Murai''' (村井 古道, [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year ({{ill|1681|en|1681|WD=Q7645|lt=1681}}) – {{ill|Kan'en|en|Kan'en|WD=Q1145723|lt=Kan'en}} 2nd year [[October 14 (Lunisolar)|October 14]] ({{ill|November 23|en|November 23|WD=Q3021|lt=November 23}}, {{ill|1749|en|1749|WD=Q7607|lt=1749}})) was a {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} [[:en:local historian|local historian]] of [[Nara]], a {{ill|surgeon|en|surgeon|WD=Q774306|lt=surgeon}}, and a [[haiku poet]].

His representative work is the ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}}''.

== Biography ==

=== Early Life ===
Kodō Murai was born in [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year (1681) in {{ill|Tōjōdo-chō|ja|東城戸町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōjōdo-chō|1=Higashijōdochō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85881228}}, Nara. His father was Michihiro Murai, the author of ''{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū|ja|南都名所集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and also a haiku poet. Kodō later lived in {{ill|Nishijōdo-chō|ja|西城戸町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishijōdo-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for much of his life.{{sfn|{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja|『俳諧今宮草』の編者無名園古道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|p=401}} His real name was Katsukuro, and as a surgeon, he used the name Shōtetsu.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=401}} Kodō’s most renowned pen name was "Mumeien Kodō," but he also used many other pseudonyms, including ''Dōsei,'' ''Suiko,'' ''Itsukawa Masuhito,'' ''Seifūken Murai Dōsei,'' and ''Sasagusa Sanjin Gengentoku Kodō.''{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=399}}

=== Work as a Haiku Poet ===
Murai was a disciple of {{ill|Konishi Raizan|ja|小西来山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konishi Raizan|1=Konishi Raizan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463609}}, editing Raizan’s posthumous collection, ''{{ill|Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja|俳諧今宮草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' and contributing to various haiku anthologies. However, despite his early exposure to haiku through his father, Murai's natural aptitude for the art form was limited, and his achievements as a haiku poet were overshadowed by his greater contributions as a local historian.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=381, 392}}

=== Interest in Local History ===
At around the age of 20, Murai became interested in local history, producing works such as his magnum opus, ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}},'' which represented a significant departure from the tradition of temple- and shrine-focused local histories.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=58}} His works incorporated a scientific approach, citing sources meticulously and critically evaluating oral traditions.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=414}}

Kodō's historical writings were considered revolutionary in Nara’s historiography. Previous works, such as ''{{ill|Nara-zoku|ja|奈良曝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara-zoku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Yamato Meisho-ki|ja|大和名所記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Meisho-ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' often lacked rigor, relying heavily on oral accounts or focusing predominantly on religious sites.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|p=414}} Kodō, however, expanded his scope to include the origins of towns and other secular topics, making ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' the first comprehensive topographical history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=413, 414}}

== Legacy and Rediscovery ==
Kodō’s works were often attributed incorrectly to his contemporary, {{ill|Hiramatsu Baichi|ja|平松倍千|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Baichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leading to centuries of confusion about his identity. This misattribution was only corrected in the mid-20th century when researchers such as Kanai Toranosuke identified Kodō as the true author of ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' and other important historical texts.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=389}}

== Works ==
=== Historical Writings ===
* ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' (奈良坊目拙解) – Murai's magnum opus and the first scientific local history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=458}}
* ''Nanto Meisho Junro Ryaku-shi'' (南都名所順路略志) – A concise guide to Nara's famous sites.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=394}}
* ''Nara Nenchū Gyōji'' (奈良年中行事) – A detailed calendar of annual events in Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=394, 395}}

=== Haiku Collections ===
* ''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa'' (俳諧今宮草) – A collection of posthumous works by Konishi Raizan, co-edited by Murai.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=381}}

== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}

== Related Articles ==

'''Kodō Murai''' (村井 古道, [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year ({{ill|1681|en|1681|WD=Q7645|lt=1681|1=1681}}) – {{ill|Kan'en|en|Kan'en|WD=Q1145723|lt=Kan'en|1=Kan'en}} 2nd year [[October 14 (Lunisolar)|October 14]] ({{ill|November 23|en|November 23|WD=Q3021|lt=November 23|1=November 23}}, {{ill|1749|en|1749|WD=Q7607|lt=1749|1=1749}})) was a {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} [[:en:local historian|local historian]] of [[Nara]], a {{ill|surgeon|en|surgeon|WD=Q774306|lt=surgeon|1=Surgeon}}, and a [[haiku poet]].

His representative work is the ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}}''.

== Biography ==

=== Early Life ===
Kodō Murai was born in [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year (1681) in {{ill|Tōjōdo-chō|ja|東城戸町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōjōdo-chō|1=Higashijōdochō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85881228}}, Nara. His father was Michihiro Murai, the author of ''{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū|ja|南都名所集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and also a haiku poet. Kodō later lived in {{ill|Nishijōdo-chō|ja|西城戸町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishijōdo-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for much of his life.{{sfn|{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja|『俳諧今宮草』の編者無名園古道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|p=401}} His real name was Katsukuro, and as a surgeon, he used the name Shōtetsu.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=401}} Kodō’s most renowned pen name was "Mumeien Kodō," but he also used many other pseudonyms, including ''Dōsei,'' ''Suiko,'' ''Itsukawa Masuhito,'' ''Seifūken Murai Dōsei,'' and ''Sasagusa Sanjin Gengentoku Kodō.''{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=399}}

=== Work as a Haiku Poet ===
Murai was a disciple of {{ill|Konishi Raizan|ja|小西来山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konishi Raizan|1=Konishi Raizan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463609}}, editing Raizan’s posthumous collection, ''{{ill|Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja|俳諧今宮草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' and contributing to various haiku anthologies. However, despite his early exposure to haiku through his father, Murai's natural aptitude for the art form was limited, and his achievements as a haiku poet were overshadowed by his greater contributions as a local historian.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=381, 392}}

=== Interest in Local History ===
At around the age of 20, Murai became interested in local history, producing works such as his magnum opus, ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96711987}},'' which represented a significant departure from the tradition of temple- and shrine-focused local histories.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=58}} His works incorporated a scientific approach, citing sources meticulously and critically evaluating oral traditions.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=414}}

Kodō's historical writings were considered revolutionary in Nara’s historiography. Previous works, such as ''{{ill|Nara-zoku|ja|奈良曝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara-zoku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Yamato Meisho-ki|ja|大和名所記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Meisho-ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' often lacked rigor, relying heavily on oral accounts or focusing predominantly on religious sites.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|p=414}} Kodō, however, expanded his scope to include the origins of towns and other secular topics, making ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' the first comprehensive topographical history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=413, 414}}

== Legacy and Rediscovery ==
Kodō’s works were often attributed incorrectly to his contemporary, {{ill|Hiramatsu Baichi|ja|平松倍千|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Baichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leading to centuries of confusion about his identity. This misattribution was only corrected in the mid-20th century when researchers such as Kanai Toranosuke identified Kodō as the true author of ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' and other important historical texts.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=389}}

== Works ==
=== Historical Writings ===
* ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' (奈良坊目拙解) – Murai's magnum opus and the first scientific local history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=458}}
* ''Nanto Meisho Junro Ryaku-shi'' (南都名所順路略志) – A concise guide to Nara's famous sites.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=394}}
* ''Nara Nenchū Gyōji'' (奈良年中行事) – A detailed calendar of annual events in Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=394, 395}}

=== Haiku Collections ===
* ''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa'' (俳諧今宮草) – A collection of posthumous works by Konishi Raizan, co-edited by Murai.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=381}}

'''Kodō Murai''' (村井 古道, [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year ({{ill|1681|en|1681|qid=Q7645|lt=1681|1=1681}}) – {{ill|Kan'en|en|Kan'en|qid=Q1145723|lt=Kan'en|1=Kan'en}} 2nd year [[October 14 (Lunisolar)|October 14]] ({{ill|November 23|en|November 23|qid=Q3021|lt=November 23|1=November 23}}, {{ill|1749|en|1749|qid=Q7607|lt=1749|1=1749}})) was a {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} [[:en:local historian|local historian]] of [[Nara]], a {{ill|surgeon|en|surgeon|qid=Q774306|lt=surgeon|1=Surgeon}}, and a [[haiku poet]].

His representative work is the ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q96711987}}''.

== Biography ==

=== Early Life ===
Kodō Murai was born in [[Tenna (Japan)|Tenna]] 1st year (1681) in {{ill|Tōjōdo-chō|ja|東城戸町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōjōdo-chō|1=Higashijōdochō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q85881228}}, Nara. His father was Michihiro Murai, the author of ''{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū|ja|南都名所集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and also a haiku poet. Kodō later lived in {{ill|Nishijōdo-chō|ja|西城戸町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishijōdo-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for much of his life.{{sfn|{{ill|Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja|『俳諧今宮草』の編者無名園古道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanto Meisho-shū Compiler|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|p=401}} His real name was Katsukuro, and as a surgeon, he used the name Shōtetsu.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=401}} Kodō’s most renowned pen name was "Mumeien Kodō," but he also used many other pseudonyms, including ''Dōsei,'' ''Suiko,'' ''Itsukawa Masuhito,'' ''Seifūken Murai Dōsei,'' and ''Sasagusa Sanjin Gengentoku Kodō.''{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=399}}

=== Work as a Haiku Poet ===
Murai was a disciple of {{ill|Konishi Raizan|ja|小西来山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konishi Raizan|1=Konishi Raizan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11463609}}, editing Raizan’s posthumous collection, ''{{ill|Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja|俳諧今宮草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haikai Ima-miya-gusa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' and contributing to various haiku anthologies. However, despite his early exposure to haiku through his father, Murai's natural aptitude for the art form was limited, and his achievements as a haiku poet were overshadowed by his greater contributions as a local historian.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|pp=381, 392}}

=== Interest in Local History ===
At around the age of 20, Murai became interested in local history, producing works such as his magnum opus, ''{{ill|Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja|奈良坊目拙解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Bōme Setsukai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 村井古道|1=Murai Kodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q96711987}},'' which represented a significant departure from the tradition of temple- and shrine-focused local histories.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=58}} His works incorporated a scientific approach, citing sources meticulously and critically evaluating oral traditions.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=414}}

Kodō's historical writings were considered revolutionary in Nara’s historiography. Previous works, such as ''{{ill|Nara-zoku|ja|奈良曝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara-zoku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' and ''{{ill|Yamato Meisho-ki|ja|大和名所記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Meisho-ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' often lacked rigor, relying heavily on oral accounts or focusing predominantly on religious sites.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|p=414}} Kodō, however, expanded his scope to include the origins of towns and other secular topics, making ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' the first comprehensive topographical history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=413, 414}}

== Legacy and Rediscovery ==
Kodō’s works were often attributed incorrectly to his contemporary, {{ill|Hiramatsu Baichi|ja|平松倍千|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Baichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leading to centuries of confusion about his identity. This misattribution was only corrected in the mid-20th century when researchers such as Kanai Toranosuke identified Kodō as the true author of ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' and other important historical texts.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=389}}

== Works ==
=== Historical Writings ===
* ''Nara Bōme Setsukai'' (奈良坊目拙解) – Murai's magnum opus and the first scientific local history of Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=458}}
* ''Nanto Meisho Junro Ryaku-shi'' (南都名所順路略志) – A concise guide to Nara's famous sites.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=394}}
* ''Nara Nenchū Gyōji'' (奈良年中行事) – A detailed calendar of annual events in Nara.{{sfn|''Nara Bōme Setsukai''|pp=394, 395}}

=== Haiku Collections ===
* ''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa'' (俳諧今宮草) – A collection of posthumous works by Konishi Raizan, co-edited by Murai.{{sfn|''Haikai Ima-miya-gusa Compiler''|p=381}}
